data.title: Trump's Liberation Day Tariffs Will Likely Change Apple Forever
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), the stock has lost 16.5% in price.

Recent "Liberation Day" tariffs by the Trump administration have significantly escalated global trade tensions. Apple depends on a highly globalized manufacturing footprint, which will face severe challenges under these measures. Investors' fears about Apple, represented in the $300 billion of market cap wiped out in a single session, outline rational concerns about margin compression, higher retail prices dampening demand, and difficulties in quickly restructuring a deeply entrenched China-based supply chain.

It is prudent for investors to maintain higher cash reserves at this time, but not all tech stocks are as vulnerable to such significant tariff impacts as Apple, leading to rational moderate bullishness amid lower valuations in supply-chain resilient tech. Unfortunately, Apple is not one such tech stock I am bullish on, foreseeing around a 17% decline in the company's stock price over the next 12 months.

Tariff Impact: Fundamental Analysis

Trump's latest Liberation Day tariffs announced by the U.S. administration impose a baseline 10% tariff on all imports, while certain countries are facing much higher measures. For example, China is facing a combined tariff of 34-54%, Vietnam 46%, the EU 20%, India 26%, and so on. Every foreign country integral to Apple's supply chain has been impacted. Apple stock plunged 8-9% in a day following this news, wiping out over $300 billion in market value. The key difference between these tariffs and the previous 2018-2019 trade-war levies is that previous tariffs largely spared Apple's core products, whereas the Liberation Day tariffs directly impact all of Apple's hardware imports.

Apple's entire international manufacturing footprint is in the crosshairs, including India and Vietnam, countries that Apple pivoted to in recent years. This is truly an extraordinary shift; these tariffs push U.S. trade barriers to the highest level in over a century. We're therefore facing immense macroeconomic uncertainty right now, and it's probably wise to have about 35% of portfolios in cash equivalents. After all, there's a heightened risk of recession. Even though equities are on sale or fairly valued now, I've been allocating to the best valuations while keeping my cash reserves high.

Fundamentally, Apple's gross margins are immediately at risk under the new tariffs. The company now faces about $39.5 billion in annual tariff costs on its imports. If Apple absorbed all these import taxes itself, it would slash operating profit by about 30-35%. The company's overall gross margin could drop from ~44% down to the mid-30s, meaning its operating margin could drop to 20% or lower.

Given these dynamics, Apple is highly unlikely to absorb 100% of these costs. Instead, the company will pass costs to customers via price hikes and find cost efficiencies to offset the tariffs. However, the big risk with price hikes is that this could sap demand, so it's unlikely that management will opt to fully neutralize the tariff effects through 30-40% price hikes. Instead, we're probably looking at up to a 15% inflation on Apple goods and the rest of the costs borne by the company's margins and offset by cost efficiencies and long-term U.S. manufacturing domestication. In other words, Apple is one such tech stock which could now be entering a prolonged bear trend over the medium term, or at least showing sideways medium-term consolidation after the 25% decline since December 2024.

Apple will probably wait until its next product refresh (the iPhone 17 launch in the fall) to quietly adjust pricing. The company will likely adopt "stealth" strategies like reducing promotions, shrinking trade-in credits, and encouraging installment plans with carrier subsidies. Apple's ability to preserve its margins will then depend on the consumer response. Either negative outcome of margin erosion or volume loss will be negative for earnings, which is why I consider the sell-off following the Liberation Day tariffs to be valid.

Tariff Impact: Manufacturing Analysis

The new Trump tariffs directly, and purposefully, undermine Apple's manufacturing and supply chain structure. The company's entire hardware business has been optimized for cost and efficiency based on globalization dynamics, and the vast majority (about 90%) of Apple's products are assembled in China. Apple does assemble a small fraction of iPhones in India and some devices in Vietnam, but these countries are now subject to U.S. tariffs too (India at 26% and Vietnam at 46%).

In the short term, Apple has very limited ability to rearrange this supply chain. Shifting production out of China at Apple's scale will take years. China remains irreplaceable in the near term due to its unparalleled manufacturing infrastructure and skilled workforce geared to electronics. The U.S. administration has deliberately left no safe harbor in India and Vietnam, so Apple will be forced to deliver a long-term manufacturing strategy in the United States if these Trump-era tariffs will continue into the next presidential term if the Republicans win with J.D. Vance.

However, there are complications to the thesis that Apple will be able to do U.S. manufacturing for its mainstream products. Apple's devices consist of hundreds of components sourced globally, so these parts will be subject to tariffs. This even applies to high-value advanced chips made in Taiwan, as TSMC (TSM) won't have its U.S. fabs ready to produce at scale for years. Manufacturing in the U.S. is also far more expensive. Building a single iPhone in the U.S. might add tens or even hundreds of dollars in cost versus building in China due to higher labor and compliance costs, primarily. For now, Apple is essentially locked into its existing China-centered supply chain.

Given these dynamics, I think Apple's near-term strategy will be to optimize margins by moving production for U.S.-bound products to lower-tariff countries. It might also push suppliers for cost concessions. Whichever route management takes, it will take extensive investment and the results will not be immediate. Apple will also surely lobby for product-specific exclusions or other relief from the U.S. government. Over the long term, Apple is almost certainly going to re-engineer its supply chain, but that's only if it deems these tariff policies as likely to persist across multiple administrations. A return to globalism from a Democrat win in four years' time could see a mass reversion of U.S. manufacturing initiatives. However, for now, Apple's China reliance has turned from an asset to a liability virtually overnight.

12-Month Price Target

If Apple's pre-tariff FY 2025 consensus net income was previously likely to be around $110 billion (around $7.30 in EPS), a $30 billion hit due to a partially offset $39.5 billion tariff bill would reduce net income to ~$80 billion. Therefore, FY 2025 GAAP EPS is likely to range between $5.50-6.00 in this base case if the company has about 15 billion shares outstanding. To be optimistic, my official base-case price target for FY 2025 is $6 in EPS.

Prior to these tariffs, Apple traded at around 26x forward earnings. Due to the current macro environment, some multiple compression is warranted. I apply a target of forward P/E of ~23x, which is close to its five-year average. If the company's EPS hits $7 for FY 2026, that will mean a stock price of about $160 within 12 months. The current stock price is $193, so I am bearish on Apple and expect a 17% decline to follow the recent tariff implementations.

Conclusion: Sell

Apple now faces significant medium-term headwinds due to the recently announced Liberation Day tariffs. Put simply, the company's historically robust margins and operational efficiency are now under threat. I expect a reduction of about $30 billion to Apple's annual net income for FY 2025. Apple's FY 2025 GAAP EPS could therefore drop to approximately $6, a significant downward revision. When applying a fair forward P/E multiple of 23x, my base-case price target is about $160 for in 12 months' time; this is roughly a 17% decline from the current price. Apple will have to respond through subtle price increases, intensified lobbying for tariff exclusions, and accelerated diversification away from China. Full near-term mitigation to ease stock price contraction appears impossible, given the complexities of restructuring the company's supply chain, which is deeply embedded in China.

Investors should expect prolonged margin pressure, medium-term share price stagnation, and most likely near-term downside. Investors should sell Apple stock, or at least not buy shares until current headwinds for the company show signs of significantly abating.

data.title: Xenon Pharmaceuticals Looks Attractive Before Epilepsy Data
data.description:
I covered Xenon Pharmaceuticals Inc. (NASDAQ:XENE) in August. Then, I noted that lead asset azetukalner (XEN1101) was running phase 3 clinical trials targeting two forms of epilepsy, focal onset seizure (FOS’) and Primary Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizures (PGTCS), and a phase 3 trial called X-NOVA2 in Major Depressive Disorder posted just last week. Azetukalner is a potassium channel opener; specifically, it is a selective Kv7.2/Kv7.3 potassium channel opener. These channels, as we will discuss shortly, play a crucial role in neuronal excitability. To be noted, Valeant and GSK plc (GSK) got approval for their Kv7 channel opener Ezogabine (Retigabine) for FOS in 2011, however, GABAergic off-target and other side effects forced it to be taken off market in 2017. Azetukalner builds on Retigabine, with efforts to modify it to avoid those side effects. It does not cause tissue discoloration. Specifically:

XEN1101 is structurally distinct from ezogabine: it has a tertiary rather than a secondary aniline compound, which prevents the formation of chromophoric phenazinium-type dimers, which are associated with ezogabine’s pigmentary clumping.

Earlier, Xenon completed the Phase 2b X-TOLE trial and is running two identical phase 3 trials based on the phase 2b data. Data from these trials will be made available by H2. The primary efficacy endpoint compares XEN1101 to placebo based on the median percent change (MPC) in monthly seizure frequency, measured from baseline through the double-blind period (DBP). The same endpoint also applies to another phase 3 trial called X-ACKT targeting PGTCS.

The molecule has excellent data in FOS from the phase 2b trial. Across all three doses, 25mg, 20mg and 10mg, Azetukalner achieved a statistically significant reduction (p<0.001) in monthly focal seizure frequency from baseline compared to placebo, meeting the primary endpoint of the trial. Reductions were 52.8% in the 25 mg group, 46.4% in the 20 mg group, and 33.2% in the 10 mg group, compared to 18.2% in the placebo group.

However, azetukalner failed to meet the primary endpoint in an MDD trial in November 2023. Specifically, the 20mg group failed to meet stat sig in the MADRS scale at week 6. Since the 25mg dose was tried and tested in FOS, they could have used it in MDD, I guess, but let us see what happened. We also need to understand this properly, so we have less doubts about whether Biohaven’s (BHVN) BHV-7000, another Kv7 channel activator, will succeed where azetukalner failed. We have to understand that MDD is a unipolar disorder, characterized by persistent depression. Unlike anxiety or bipolar disorders, it has no symptoms of mania or excitability.

Now, both azetukalner and BHV-7000 are highly selective for Kv7.2/7.3, although the degree of selectivity and specificity may vary. Now, see what research says:

Kv7 (“M-type,” KCNQ) K+ currents play dominant roles in controlling neuronal excitability. They act as a “brake” against hyperexcitable states in the central and peripheral nervous systems.

This is also called the M-current. When impairment in the Kv7 channel reduces M-current activity, neurons become excitable and this causes hyperexcitability, symptoms of which are part of bipolar disorder. Thus, augmenting M-current by opening the Kv7 channels could reduce hyperexcitability and, in turn, address one key aspect of bipolar disorder. However, it is not as well-researched how opening the kv7 channel does the opposite thing, modulates excitability and counters depression. It is not even clearly known how excitability is related to depression.

Bottom line, where azetukalner failed, there is no certainty that BHV-7000 will succeed in depression. The two molecules are different, but not in this way. Their basic difference lies in the fact that while azetukalner is a legacy compound of retigabine and may inherit its GABAergic off-target side effects (Xenon researchers have actively worked to stop this from happening), BHV-7000 is a de novo scaffold not at all based on retigabine (so it will not inherit retigabine’s problems, but may possibly come with some of its own).

I should note that preclinical data saw a link between Kv7 channels and depression. Here’s what it said in preclinical tests of azetukalner:

Pre-clinical work demonstrates a regulatory role for the KCNQ-type potassium channels in reversing depressive phenotypes following social defeat stress. Results from clinical studies using the first generation, Kv7 potassium channel modulator, ezogabine, supported the hypothesis that increasing KCNQ channel signaling can be beneficial for patients with depression and anhedonia.

The key idea here is the relation between hyperexcitability and depression...

...dampening the hyperexcitability of the dopamine neurons that is associated with depressive/anhedonia phenotypes observed in the susceptible mice.

Anyhow, trials will tell us if this hypothesis is true. Note that there is hope because the MADRS data at one week was statistically significant, so there was an early onset of effect, but it did not last. The phase 2 trial is also using 20mg. However, it is now using the HAMD-17 instead of MADRS.

HAMD-17 is an older metric; it is broader and sensitive to four factors - “an "anxiety" factor, a "depression" factor, an "insomnia" factor, and a "somatic" factor.” HAMD-17 has weightage towards somatic and anxiety symptoms, which are more relevant to a Kv7 channel opener but somewhat less relevant to core depression symptoms. MADRS, on the other hand, focuses on core mood symptoms, including sadness, pessimism, anhedonia, and concentration issues. It also has four factors - “a “sadness” factor, a neurovegetative factor, a “detachment” factor and a “negative thoughts” factor.” MADRS is increasingly replacing HAMD-17 in clinical trials, although HAMD-17 is still used. For example, the recent approval of zuranalone (in 2023) was based on a clinical trial whose primary outcome measure was HAMD-17 score in patients with postpartum depression.

Another molecule, Gepirone, was approved last year for MDD. It had been in trials for over two decades, and its approval was based on old trials using the HAMD-17 score. Interestingly, in some trials, at least, where MADRS was used, gepirone failed - see table 1, here. All of that doesn’t mean much, except that in choosing HAMD-17 over MADRS, Xenon is possibly giving azetukalner some small advantage in the phase 3 MDD trial. Interestingly, in the phase 2 trial in MDD that failed, the HAMD-17 secondary endpoint was met, but barely:

Statistical significance was achieved on the pre-specified endpoint of the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, or HAM-D17, at week 6 with a mean reduction of 10.18 in the placebo group and 13.26 in the XEN1101 20 mg group (p=0.042).

Other Updates

Meanwhile, azetukalner continued posting even more positive data for the FOS program. In December, an OLE study saw a long-term, sustained duration of treatment effects. For the key data, let me just quote Dr. Christopher Kenney, the CMO of Xenon:

We are excited …[to]... share new long-term azetukalner data from our ongoing X-TOLE open-label extension study showing compelling evidence of sustained seizure reduction and seizure freedom rates…Approximately one-third of patients who have been on azetukalner for at least 36 months achieved 100% seizure reduction, or seizure freedom, for a period of one year or longer. …we continue to see an impressive, sustained monthly reduction in seizure frequency – approximately 85% at month 36 – while importantly maintaining a consistent safety profile that suggests azetukalner continues to be generally well-tolerated.

The company also notes that seizure freedom is a meaningful metric in FOS studies because it “translates directly into improved quality of life for people living with epilepsy.” As researchers have observed:

Previous research suggests that seizure freedom may be necessary to improve health-related quality of life (HRQOL) for epilepsy surgery patients, but little is known regarding the seizure-frequency reduction needed to improve HRQOL among medically treated individuals….Changes over time in overall QOLIE-31 and QOLIE-89 scores were significantly more positive for subjects who achieved seizure freedom (i.e., 100% reduction in seizure frequency) than for those who did not….In this study, HRQOL improvement occurred primarily among patients who achieved complete seizure freedom. Many AED trials use a 50% seizure-frequency reduction criterion as a trial end point, but measurable impacts of this degree of reduction in seizure frequency on HRQOL in this sample were not observed. These results further support striving for seizure freedom as an epilepsy care goal.

Thus, azetukalner met the epilepsy care goal completely in over one-third of the patients, those that achieved 100% seizure freedom, at 36 months. For a quick competition analysis, Eisai’s approved drug Fycompa and SK Pharma’s Xcopri data are as follows:

Fycompa - In this global pooled analysis, among the adult epilepsy patients with effectiveness assessed by seizure type, patients with focal seizures only, had seizure freedom and responder rates of 14.5% and 44.6% at the last visit, respectively. [Fycompa had 2024 net product revenue of $137.3 million]

Xcopri - 13.1% had complete (100%) seizure reduction. [Xcopri 2023 US net sales was $202.8 million, 2024 was "north of $320 million"].

There are nuances in these data points that are difficult to compare directly, but azetukalner has done very well in FOS, there is no doubt about that. Last time, I noted that there may be 2.5-2.8 million FOS patients in the US alone. Current standards of treatment are ASM drugs that do not address the disease directly. The global epilepsy market is ~$12bn in size, and since FOS accounts for roughly 60% of seizures, the global FOS market can be pegged at ~$7bn.

I should also note what the management says about the points of differentiation with Xcopri:

If you take a look at the seizure freedom data that they frequently talk about, it's usually at a dose 400 milligrams a day, which isn't really used that much. When you take a look at our seizure freedom data that we're talking about and our open label extension study, those patients are on 20 milligrams. So our go to dose in Phase 3 is 25 milligrams.

So it's in the range of a dose that we expect to be used down the road. Sherry made a comment to the allergic reaction. We haven't had any cases of DRESS. So I think that we have more to learn about this drug and to get it approved. Based upon the data so far, I think that we stack up pretty well from a seizure freedom perspective. We don't have the titration which Sherry mentioned.

Financials

XENE has a market cap of $2.41bn and a cash and ST investments balance of $626.9mn, while cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities were $754.4 million as of December 31, 2024. R&D and G&A expenses were $59.5mn and $18mn, respectively. Their CFO was ($54.8mn) and capex was ($0.8mn), giving them an FCF of -$55.6mn, and a cash runway of 11 quarters if we consider the first cash balance value, and 2 more quarters if you consider the second value.

Risks

The global MDD market, at ~$7bn forecast for 2034, is smaller than the FOS market, and it has many more drugs. So FOS is a good target, but MDD is not a poor choice for indication either. However, data here is not as convincing as with FOS, so XENE may miss out on an opportunity here.

Apart from that, increasing competition from the likes of Biohaven may reduce the competitive space. If BHV-7000 continues to show a clean safety profile - and if azetukalner fails in this regard along the way - that will be bad for the company’s prospects. However, that seems like a distant cause for worry now, given its good AE profile seen so far.

Bottom Line

Xenon Pharmaceuticals Inc. stock looks increasingly attractive as the date for the topline data for the FOS trial approaches. True, its MDD program is still shaky, but the FOS program alone makes it interesting. I will consider a buy at current 52-week low prices.

data.title: Paramount Resources: Forget The Past
data.description:
When Paramount Resources Ltd. (OTCPK:PRMRF) announced a big deal that the last article covered, it made the recent fourth quarter report obsolete before anyone could read it. About all that was relevant was that the deal closed as planned. Management announced a special cash dividend. Now, going forward, the situation is entirely new as properties that were a small part of the total company production now are the company production. The company remains debt-free with a lot of cash. But management intends to "hit the ground running" to get the production back up to roughly half the level it was probably within twelve months.

Current Company Summary

Shown below is what the new company is beginning with.

(Note: Paramount Resources is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted).

To the extent the events of the fourth quarter and the first quarter of the new fiscal year got all of us to the current point shown above, those reports are relevant. But once the company received the cash, large amounts of the fourth quarter report were replaced by that cash. The result is shown above.

Management has made clear that an unusually large amount of the capital budget will be for infrastructure to support the company growth that will follow. Therefore, production growth is backend-loaded. There is a processing plant under construction that is needed before some wells can be brought into production (for example).

Now once that infrastructure is in place, then the growth planned for 2026 is realistic.

Paramount had long found plays before those plays became important to the industry and acquired a position at a low cost. But the real consideration was that the company was able to pick the acreage it wanted because much of the industry was not paying attention to the play. Management then sells the developed acreage (like it just did) for a considerable premium to the cost while making good money every step of the way.

The difference this time around is that the company remained essentially debt-free throughout the whole process. It looks as though the intention is to keep the debt very low or debt-free well into the future. In some ways, this is a kind of cyclical growth in that most of the production gets sold while retaining ever more production to end up with a larger company each time.

While building production, management has a history of selling parcels for good gains to fund the company's growth. Elsewhere in the corporate presentation, management has a few million acres to work with. So, the company will not be running out of acreage any time soon.

Prospect Summary

Basically, the company removed any slow growth or no-growth acreage from the portfolio.

If anything is sold before the next cycle is completed, it is likely to be Kaybob North. The immediate significant growth will be the Willesden Green which will (at least initially) be where most of the production is.

Clearly, this acreage will be a cash flow engine for funding some other prospects noted above once this acreage reaches the intended production levels.

But this also means that this acreage is likely going to be above average in profitability and cash flow. This was a company that both paid a decent dividend and grew production. Management appears to have the same idea in mind for the prospects shown above. Accordingly, this should be a growth and income prospect with a bit more growth than usual.

Even though the new dividend was reduced from previous levels, investors received a lot of cash to reinvest. That cash should have investors at about the same place (when reinvested) as they were before the transaction.

This is the other potential growth opportunity for the immediate future. In both cases, the company may own other intervals that could prove to be commercial in the future. But those intervals are not the current emphasis.

Future Development

Probably of all the future prospects, the one that has the most industry attention by far would be the heavy oil and thermal oil prospects.

The Clearwater Play has now been well-established by many other companies I follow as a very profitable place to operate. The industry is now in the process of expanding that profitability to the other areas shown above.

Since there have been many instances of success, this area could well prove to be a below-average exploration risk for the company in the future.

Until now, this company has not really ventured into the heavy oil and thermal oil business. But the profitability of the area could change that proposition.

Summary

Paramount Resources has long sold most of its production for a good price while retaining some solid future prospects. Two areas have production and will be the first to maximize prospects. However, the company has other solid exploration projects that are likely to succeed as well.

North America is one of the most explored continents in the world. Therefore, much of the oil and gas deposits are already known. What is new is the technology to profitably exploit some of these areas that were too costly to develop.

The company remains a strong buy based on its historical record of finding above-average profitability prospects that allowed it to pay a dividend while growing production. Investors should expect the emphasis on exploration and developing growth projects to continue. When projects head into the mature (and slow growth) phase, they will be likely sold. Usually, those sales generate sizable gains as was the case with the latest sale.

Risks

The current political uncertainty surrounding the threats of tariffs and trade wars is assumed to be a bargaining stance that will be replaced by something that is both permanent and constructive. But there is no assurance that will happen.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn will likely materially change the future of the company. This company generally maintains a debt-free balance sheet with very low breakeven costs. Therefore, others may suffer a lot more in the industry before this company has any financial headaches during a cyclical downturn.

A loss of key personnel could materially set back the company's future plans.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs Are Positive For O'Reilly Automotive
data.description:
Introduction

The recently announced 25% tariffs on imported vehicles and auto parts is already going into effect, and it has major implications for auto manufacturers, car dealers, and consumers.

At first sight, tariffs on auto parts don't sound good for O'Reilly Automotive (NASDAQ:ORLY), since higher costs could hurt margins.

However, the company has not only resisted the recent selloff on the stock market but is trading at all-time highs.

In this article, I will share my view on why tariffs are positive for O'Reilly Automotive, and why I keep holding my position (~7.5% of the portfolio).

Context

Starting April 2, President Donald Trump has imposed a 25% tariff on all passenger vehicles and light trucks. In a month's time, the same rate will be applied to auto parts.

For those vehicles coming from Canada and Mexico, the tariff will only be applied to foreign parts that make up the vehicle.

While some car manufacturers are more affected than others by this policy, depending on the percentage produced in the U.S. and the sourcing of their parts, it will affect the whole auto industry.

Of the 16 million cars sold in the U.S. last year, half were imported ($264B). Even for the ones manufactured in the U.S., less than 50% of its parts are sourced from the country.

After tariffs take effect, the competitive position of manufacturers that assemble a higher percentage in the U.S. and import fewer parts will improve, at the expense of those who source a higher percentage from abroad.

However, even those brands that manufacture a high percentage of its vehicles in the U.S. such as Tesla (TSLA) or Honda, ~30% of the components are imported.

Tariffs Are Positive For O'Reilly Automotive

Tariffs on auto parts per se are not directly positive for O'Reilly, but higher prices for new cars are.

With the effect of tariffs, imported cars become more expensive, since automakers don't have enough margins to take the full hit of tariffs, and they will be forced to pass it to customers.

Estimating the exact increase in prices is difficult due to the many moving parts, including consumer behavior, tariff absorption across the value chain, relocation of manufacturing facilities, or a decrease in imports, among others.

However, estimates range from an increase of $2,500 for the lowest-cost U.S.-manufactured cars, up to $15,000 for SUVs and luxury cars.

On average, this could translate into a ~15% price increase for new cars, and in consequence, a decline in new vehicle sales, which are currently at its high end.

In most cases, buying a new car is discretionary spending, and consumers can postpone renewing to a new model and keep driving their current car for a few more years.

The increase in the average age of vehicles in the U.S. has been a secular trend going on for decades. In 1995, the average passenger car was 8.4 years old. It increased to 11 years in 2012, and then 12.6 years in 2024.

Although there is a ceiling on the average age per vehicle, and I suspect it might be close, an increase in new car prices will keep the average age per vehicle at high levels.

As vehicles age, they lose coverage by the manufacturer's warranty and require higher maintenance costs, benefiting O'Reilly's revenues.

Stable Margins

While demand for replacement parts will increase, supply costs will also. So the obvious question is, how will the situation affect margins?

O'Reilly has over 645 suppliers and imports around 25% of its products from China, 17% from Mexico, and 3% from Canada.

However, an undisclosed percentage of the products sourced from the U.S. have been imported by a distributor. So even if O'Reilly does not directly import them, they are also affected by tariffs.

In that situation, O'Reilly will have to negotiate with suppliers what percentage is absorbed by them, trying to leverage its purchasing power. Also, the company expects to pass these price increases to customers:

I really feel like we're as well positioned as anyone to effectively deal with those challenges and to work with our supplier partners to be able to pass along anything in a very rational industry that may come our way.

Source: Brent Kirby (President), Q4 2024 Earnings Call.

Due to the low demand elasticity for most of its products, in my view, O'Reilly will be able to increase prices without having a significant effect on volumes.

Discretionary products such as wax, wipers, or accessories represent a small percentage of O'Reilly's business, and customers will still need to replace the batteries, starters, and tires even if it is at higher prices.

To prove the point, let's take a look at how the company performed in previous periods of tariff enhancements.

During the last round of tariff increases by the Trump Administration in 2018 and 2019, O'Reilly not just kept its gross margins, but increased them.

It is important to note that O'Reilly uses the last-in, first-out (LIFO) method to account for the cost of goods sold, so the company was not benefiting from the sale of old inventory.

Looking at the comments from O'Reilly's biggest competitor's management, AutoZone (AZO), I don't expect a price war in the industry, especially taking into account they operate in an oligopoly.

There are several outcomes that may impact our go-forward results, including vendor absorption of the higher costs, diversifying our sourcing, which we've done in the past, taking pricing actions or some combination of the 3.

But to be clear, we intend to maintain our margin profile post tariffs, and we expect the entire industry will behave in a rational way, just as our historical experience has shown.

Source: Jamere Jackson (CFO), AutoZone's Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

Expected Growth

Before the tariff announcement, O'Reilly guided for 2% to 4% same-store revenue growth.

As higher prices are passed to customers, especially during the second half of the year, revenues could increase at the high-end of guidance.

On top of the 4% organic growth, the store count will increase above 3% during 2025 due to the 200-210 new store openings. Store count growth will be mainly driven by the expansion in Mexico, with a 29% increase.

In Canada, where O'Reilly recently expanded through the acquisition of Vasto Auto, management does not expect a significant expansion during 2025, but it will be accelerated in the following year.

All things considered, I expect O'Reilly to deliver ~100bp above the analyst consensus in revenue growth.

Assuming stable operating margins of around 19.5% and a 2.5% decrease in share count, in line with O'Reilly's long-term strategy of share buybacks, earnings per share (EPS) will grow by 9% to $44.3 during FY2025.

Valuation

From a long-term perspective, I forecast a 7% revenue growth based on store count growth of 3%-3.5% plus inflation. After 2030, I assume a decline in store count growth rates.

Considering O'Reilly's operating leverage and scale efficiencies, operating margins will keep increasing by 20 bps per year going forward.

Based on a 7.5% discount rate, a 4% growth in perpetuity, and a 31x last-twelve months FCF exit multiple (10-year average), the stock is currently fairly priced.

Considering the current valuation multiples at the high end of its historic range, I have adjusted the share count decrease to 3% per year.

While it is lower than in the past, and buybacks will remain the primary way of returning excess cash to shareholders, the company is being constrained by valuation.

As O'Reilly beats expectations due to higher revenues and EPS, and the stock price digests the above-average valuation multiples, it should deliver a 7.6% CAGR looking forward (accounting for a decrease in valuation).

Although I acknowledge the increase in valuation, I consider the stock to be fairly valued based on the presented assumptions, with a fair price of $1,410 per share. Due to the defensiveness of the business model, the high degree of predictability, and the tariff catalyst, I still hold my shares and assign a Buy rating.

Risks

Despite the defensiveness of O'Reilly's business model, some risks could challenge the investment thesis.

Spike In Inflation

If inflation rates rise above a certain level, which I estimate is around 6% to 10%, gross margins would be impacted.

Even if the company can pass price increases on to customers, above a certain level the customer is forced to shift towards cheaper products, which carry lower margins.

Also, wages would need to be increased and the assumptions I presented in my model regarding operating margins should be revised.

As an example, we can review the performance under high inflation rates, as we recently had this scenario during 2022.

As shown in the chart above, O'Reilly had a decrease in gross margins when inflation spiked above 6%.

Supply Chain Disruptions

The other main risk would be a disruption in supply chains.

If tariffs generate a chaotic situation in international trade and supply chains get disrupted, some products might become unavailable.

One of the major competitive advantages of O'Reilly is its wide range of products, the high amount of stock keeping units it can provide (~153,000 SKUs), and its distribution network.

If some parts become unavailable, volumes would decrease, impacting revenues.

Takeaway

In conclusion, if inflation rates remain below 6%, O'Reilly is one of the biggest beneficiaries of the recently announced tariffs on vehicle imports.

Tariffs will drive an increase in prices and lower new car sales, which will translate into higher average vehicle age and maintenance spending.

In my view, the company will deliver better than expected revenue growth in 2025, and the long-term investment thesis remains strong.

Although the stock is fairly priced and does not offer a discount to its fair value, the defensiveness and predictability of its business model make it a perfect fit for uncertain times.

The low beta of the stock helps to balance most portfolios, and I will hold my shares unless it gets significantly overvalued. For now, it is a buy.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition – open to all analysts – is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Processing Visa's Outperformance
data.description:
When it comes to the payment processing industry, Visa Inc. (NYSE:V) is the 800-pound gorilla in the room. As of this writing, the company boasts a market capitalization of $669.78 billion. This makes the enterprise the 15th largest publicly traded firm on the planet. In fact, with the exception of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), the company is the largest publicly traded company on the planet that is focused solely on the financial industry. Well, earlier this year, I decided to revisit the business. At that time, the stock had been experiencing some nice upside compared to the broader market. However, because of how pricey the shares were, I could not in good conscience rate the company any better than a "hold."

From that time through today, the business has surprisingly continued to outperform the market. Driven by attractive revenue, profit, and cash flow growth, Visa has seen its share price climb by 11.2% at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) is down 4.7%. As great as this upside is to see if you are a shareholder, the company remains expensive on an absolute basis, almost to the point of justifying a downgrade instead of an upgrade. I don't intend to take that step today. But I do think investors in the firm would be wise to continue monitoring it closely. Especially during these uncertain times, it doesn't take much bad news to push shares lower.

Great performance

Fundamentally speaking, there is no denying that Visa is doing better than ever right now. For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, which is the most recent quarter for which we have data that's available, the company reported revenue of $9.51 billion. This represented an increase of 10.1% compared to the $8.63 billion reported the year before. During this time, the company benefited from continued growth. The number of cards that it has outstanding grew from roughly 4.5 billion to 4.7 billion. It also enjoyed a rather significant increase in the number of transactions from 57.47 billion to 63.80 billion. That's a year-over-year increase of 11%.

Obviously, the number of cards outstanding certainly helps achieve transaction growth. But the firm's revenue is tied to more than just that. Another contributor is the volume of cash transferred or paid. During the first quarter of 2025, Visa achieved a volume of $4.15 trillion. That's up materially from the $3.52 trillion reported the previous year. It is worth mentioning that it's the previous quarter that is responsible for generating the company's revenue. So the final quarter of 2024 is what really matters for the first quarter of 2025. During the final quarter of last year, volume was a bit lower at $4.03 trillion. However, that was still 5.2% above the $3.83 trillion reported the previous year. I don't know about you, but that is a mind-boggling amount of money to me. However, this does well to illustrate the sheer enormity of the business.

*Mastercard data here. American Express data here.

To put it in perspective how large the company is, we need only look at how it stacks up against some other similar companies such as Mastercard Incorporated (MA) and American Express Company (AXP). In the chart above, you can see how the three firms look in relation to one another when it comes to the number of cards that they have outstanding. And in the chart below, you can see how they look in terms of total volumes processed. It is clear to me who the big player in this space is just based on these comparisons alone.

The rise in revenue brought with it higher profits and cash flows as well. Net income, for instance, totaled $5.12 billion during the first quarter of 2025. That's 4.7% greater than the $4.89 billion the business reported the previous year. There are, of course, other profitability metrics that we should be looking at also. Operating cash flow is one of them. It jumped from $3.61 billion to $5.40 billion in the span of a year. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $9.05 billion to $9.56 billion. Furthermore, there is also EBITDA. From the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025, this metric popped from $6.21 billion to $6.81 billion.

I would make the case that it is still quite early to project out with the rest of this year might look like. Having said that, if we use the historical figures achieved for the last two completed fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how Visa is priced on an absolute basis. That chart also compares the company to both Mastercard and American Express. As a note, seeing as how the corporate structure of American Express differs materially from these other two players, it does not have an EV to EBITDA multiple. What we can see from looking at all of this is that Visa is priced in between its two competitors. This further indicates to me that while shares are expensive on an absolute basis, the business is probably fairly valued on a relative basis.

There are some other things that we should take into consideration when evaluating the company. For starters, as I detailed in my previous article, Visa has a history of buying back stock. In the most recent quarter, the company repurchased 13 million shares for a total of $3.94 billion. That is admittedly down from the 15 million shares that the company picked up for $3.61 billion during the first quarter of 2024. But even so, this is a large amount of cash being deployed for a large quantity of shares. Given how shares are priced at the moment, I would much prefer that management allocate this capital toward growth initiatives. However, investors should not confuse large amounts of buybacks with an absence of innovation.

Pretty regularly, the management team at Visa comes out with news that details how core aspects of the business are performing. As an example, one thing that the firm has been pushing for has been tap to phone transactions. As management described it,

"Tap to Phone is a tech equalizer for businesses. Walk into some of the world's largest retailers or go to your local farmers market and they're using this same technology to accept payments right on their phone."

This may not seem like much, but what's most valuable for the brand is to create product stickiness. This is done by making the product more appealing to use and easier to use. According to management, in a press release issued on March 3rd, the tap to phone feature saw a 200% increase in usage over just the past year. In some countries like the US, the UK, and Brazil, there was an aggregate rise of 234%. Almost 30% of all sellers offering this optionality our new small businesses. Management views this as a way to further cater to the small and medium-sized enterprises that it certainly hopes will become the big players in the economy tomorrow.

Management followed this up with a press release on March 11th where they talked about a new scam disruption practice aimed at protecting consumers across the globe. This is a new department that the company has that it claims has already saved victims $350 million spread across dozens of scams just in the past year. This doesn't even factor in the $40 billion that its Visa Payment Ecosystem Risk and Control operation blocked in attempted fraud on the firm's network in the last year.

Over the last five years, the firm has invested over $12 billion in technology, with at least some of this going toward helping to reduce fraud and improve the firm's overall network security. Building trust and promoting security is absolutely instrumental for a company such as this. I fully expect initiatives like these to continue to roll out from the company.

Takeaway

At this time, I think that Visa Inc. is a fantastic business. But frankly, it's trading at a price that I cannot justify. I wouldn't go so far as to downgrade it to a "sell" because of how high quality the business is how well it's growing. Having said that, shares are expensive enough on an absolute basis to justify maintaining a "hold" rating for now.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals: 3-Drug Company Reaches Profitability
data.description:
When I last wrote about Rigel Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RIGL) in late 2023, I rated it a strong buy due to a narrowing net loss, cheap valuation based on price/sales multiple and catalysts for its pipeline ahead. Since that time, RIGL has traded up roughly 60% with the company reporting a net income of $17.5M for 2024. An overhanging patent lawsuit with Annora Pharma concerning RIGL's biggest earner Tavalisse (fostamatinib), was a concern I noted at the time, although I expected that to take time to resolve. This article looks at recent developments for RIGL and the road ahead.

Tavalisse remains important

RIGL is a three-drug company with Tavalisse (fostamatinib), Rezlidhia (olutasidenib) and Gavreto (pralsetinib) as its marketed drugs. Tavalisse is still the big earner, however, accounting for two thirds ($31M) of RIGL's net product sales in Q4'24 ($46.5M).

The news on March 27, 2025, that RIGL had settled with Annora Pharma, in their patent litigation concerning Tavalisse, is important to digest. RIGL has agreed to let Annora sell a generic fostamatinib in the US in Q2'2032, or possibly earlier, which could mean RIGL might not face generic competition in the US for another seven years!

Under the terms of the settlement agreement, Annora will have a license to sell its generic product in Q2 2032 or earlier under certain circumstances. In accordance with the agreement, the parties terminated all ongoing litigation between Rigel and Annora regarding TAVALISSE patents pending in New Jersey.

RIGL press release March 27, 2025.

Language relating to "certain circumstances" allowing for an earlier entry to market is not uncommon when a company announces a settlement following a patent lawsuit. For example, such language was included in a January 2025, press release from Intra-Cellular Therapies (ITCI), when ITCI settled with Sandoz (OTCQX:SDZNY), allowing SDZNY to sell a generic Caplyta (lumateperone) in 2040.

The settlement agreement permits Sandoz to begin selling generic versions of CAPLYTA on July 1, 2040, or earlier under certain circumstances.

ITCI press release, January 10, 2025.

The possibility for earlier entry than the stated date in these agreements could refer to an "acceleration clause" or "most-favored entry clause." The exact terms of any such clause in the agreement between Annora and RIGL won't likely be known to us any time soon, but its mere presence shouldn't be of concern. Indeed, in January, the Federal Trade Commission issued a report, analyzing pharmaceutical patent settlement agreements from FY2018-FY2021, covered by the Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement and Modernization Act of 2003 (MMA 2003). That report noted that in FY2021, the most recent year we have data from, of 199 settlements filed under the MMA 2003, 166 of them contained acceleration provisions.

Indeed, in the ITCI example above, the fact that the settlement with SDZNY covering Caplyta appeared to include some sort of acceleration provision didn't stop Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) from announcing it had agreed to acquire ITCI a few days later.

RIGL readouts ahead

RIGL is continuing on with development of R289 for lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (LR-MDS), but that work has been going on for years, such as initial trials of R835, of which R289 is a pro-drug, that began in 2018. Dose escalation of R289 has already seen some LR-MDS patients achieve transfusion independence, however, and so we're certainly at a point where RIGL might begin producing something more compelling to the market.

RIGL is currently dosing patients at 500 mg of R289, twice a day, and plans to start dose expansion work later in 2025. It looks like another readout from the phase 1b study can be expected in H2'25, so that represents a 2025 clinical catalyst for RIGL.

For our R289 program in lower-risk MDS, we expect to complete the dose escalation portion of the Phase Ib study. The new six dose level, 500 milligrams twice daily is actively enrolling. We then plan to initiate the dose expansion phase later this year. Also during the year, we plan to seek health authority input on the registrational path for R289 and we're anticipating presenting the dose escalation data from the Phase Ib study in the second half of the year.

Lisa Rojkjaer, Chief Medical Officer at RIGL, Q4'24 earnings call.

With olutasidenib, trials in various indications are now enrolling in concert with MD Anderson, but might take time to produce some data, RIGL hasn't provided much guidance on that front.

With the RIPK1 inhibitor program partnered with Eli Lilly and Co (LLY), LLY is running a trial of ocadusertib in an estimated 380 rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients according to the clinicaltrials.gov listing. That study is randomized, double-blind and placebo-controlled, and has an efficacy endpoint at 12-weeks, so it should produce a fairly useful picture of how active ocadusertib is in RA patients. While the trial is still enrolling, it has an estimated primary completion date of February 2026, so it isn't years away from producing what could be a very big result for RIGL.

RIGL's Corporate presentation also mentions "in-licensing and product acquisition" as a potential strategy for development and expansion of its business (slide 3). With the company reporting a net income for 2024 and Q4'24 the possibility of in-licensing something to add to its three drugs is plausible, but whether or not that happens in 2025 remains to be seen. RIGL might prefer to see whether or not LLY chooses to develop ocadusertib further first, which could trigger milestone payments, making an in-licensing or acquisition even more feasible.

Financial Overview

RIGL reported total revenues of $57.6M in Q4'24 including $46.5M in net product sales and a further $11.1M in contract revenue from collaborations. Cost of product sales were $5.8M in Q4'24, R&D expenses were $5.6M for Q4'24 and SG&A expenses were $29.5M in the same quarter. RIGL reported a net income of $14.3M for the quarter or $0.81 per share.

As of December 31, 2024, RIGL had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $77.3M. The company does have an outstanding credit facility with MidCap, with a principal balance of $60M, $7.5M of which is due in 2025.

Nonetheless, RIGL is guiding for 2025 total revenues of $200M-$210M, and net income for 2025. With that considered, RIGL's cash is not currently a concern, I don't see the company as likely to have to raise funds here.

There were 17,862,958 shares of RIGL's common stock outstanding as of February 25, 2025. RIGL has a market cap of $315.5M.

Conclusions, rating and risks

RIGL has reported a net income for the full year 2024, and for Q4'24. Even Tavalisse continues to grow, over six years from its launch. The recent settlement with Annora should help allay concerns over RIGL's dependency on Tavalisse currently. In any case, RIGL has the ability to continue to grow beyond Tavalisse with Gavreto off to a good start and Rezlidhia beginning key trials in new indications. There is also the RIPK1 inhibitor program in partnership with LLY that could greatly change RIGL's fortunes, given the size of the market for RA drugs. Considering RIGL's profitability, potential for further revenue growth, and the Annora settlement, I rate RIGL a buy. I stop short of rating the name a strong buy as RIGL could experience a dip in Q1 in net product sales, as it did in 2024, and Q1'25 earnings are a near-term event.

The risks of any long in RIGL are several fold, a few of which I'll discuss. Firstly, RIGL speaks of the possibility of in-licensing and product acquisition, and the company has done well before picking up Rezlidhia and Gavreto, but there are no guarantees that the market will be impressed by any subsequent in-licensing/acquisition.

Secondly, RIGL has reported sales growth of its marketed drugs over time, but the company still experiences seasonal variation in sales. A weak quarter could lead the market to question if RIGL can meet its 2025 revenue guidance.

Lastly, RIGL could report delays in starting or completing its clinical trials, as could its partners, such as LLY. Delays would move potential label expansion into the future and could also cause the stock to tumble.

data.title: Nasdaq Is In A Technical Bear Market: History Tells Us Where The Bottom Could Be
data.description:
Nobody could have expected what we've been experiencing in the last 48 hours: Liberation Day will go down in history for better or for worse. China responded to the U.S. tariff, and the result was once again devastating all over the world: The MSCI World Index is plummeting by over 4.20%.

Very few companies are holding up; technology companies have been the hardest hit.

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has plummeted 22% from its all-time high, and the S&P 500 (SP500) will probably follow suit. After all, the major U.S. tech companies represent 1/3 of the index and the well-known threshold of -20% from the high is quite close.

In addition to China's retaliatory tariffs, the market fears an even more aggressive response from Trump, who has already stated that “China played it wrong.” In all this, the European Union's reaction to the tariffs is still pending, and will probably come in the next few days. All in all, the trade war is heating up and heading towards an escalation.

Volatility is huge and investors are concerned about how quickly the situation is deteriorating. But how far can the U.S. stock market crash go? Nobody knows the answer, but we can get an idea based on what has happened in the past.

What history tells us and what I think

Firstly, a 20% drop does not necessarily translate into a recession, nor even two consecutive quarters of GDP contraction. The National Bureau of Economic Research decides when a recession starts and ends; there are many other economic aspects to consider, not just technical ones. It is important to specify this aspect because if the market collapses by 20%, it is not certain that there would be a recession that will lead to a collapse of a greater magnitude. We have a recent example of this, namely 2022.

The S&P 500 fell by 27%, but then began to rise again and there was no recession during that period. The index was simply too overvalued, and inflationary pressures combined with the Russo-Ukrainian war exacerbated the macroeconomic context. Everyone expected a recession, but it never happened, probably because the huge fiscal deficit supported economic growth.

However, let's assume that this time it's different, and that the United States is really heading towards a recession due to the trade war. How far could this collapse go? A study by Kathmere Capital Management helps us understand it.

Firstly, considering all the recessions since the 1950s, from its all-time high, the S&P 500 took an average of 13.60 months to reach its lowest point and fell by an average of 30.80%. Following these guidelines, the bottom should be reached on March 25, 2026, at 4,276 points, about 17% below current levels. A few weeks ago it seemed impossible, today a little less so.

However, considering all the recessions since the 1950s, the outliers of 1980 and 2020 could skew the final result. After all, Covid-19 was a flash crash and the performance of the S&P 500 in 2020 was very positive overall. I want to be as pessimistic as possible and consider only the most recent and severe recessions, i.e., 2001 and 2007. In this case, the bottom will be reached on February 10, 2027, at 2,887 points. This means that the S&P 500 must lose a further 43%, so we're really only just getting started. Obviously, this is an almost impossible scenario, it would require a black swan event.

However, there is some positive news in all this: two years after the recession began, you have probably already recovered a significant part of your losses.

On average, two years after the start of a recession, the S&P 500 is up 32%. There may be very negative periods, such as in 2007 and 2001, but if you're patient enough and average the price down during the crash, you'll reach break-even faster. In other words, by investing during the downturn and keeping your nerve, it's just a matter of several months/quarters. It will certainly be a stressful period, but it will end: bear markets tend to last much less than bull markets.

Finally, let's take a look at the Shiller PE Ratio, a famous indicator of market valuation. I will consider the last 20 years since I don't think it makes much sense to compare the current stock market with that of the last century.

As you can see, we are still at a rather high level, but today's sharp drop has not been counted. Even including it, we are above average and still far from the levels reached in 2022. Frankly, I don't think we'll ever reach 15x like during the GFC, reaching 25x would already be a decent level to believe that the bottom has been reached.

I don't have a crystal ball and I can't know where the bottom will be, but I have marked four potential levels based on different scenarios. They are just personal considerations, nothing more.

  • The first scenario is that the bottom is at the current level, and the market will start to recover in the coming weeks. Trump will back down on the tariffs, as will China, and the European Union will try to negotiate without adding fuel to the fire.
  • The second scenario, which I consider more likely, is that Trump will continue along these lines for a while longer, and the European Union could come into play with a plan of retaliatory tariffs. At that point, a new collapse will be triggered, causing the S&P 500 to lose 21%.

    After a few weeks of negotiations everything will be resolved as far as tariffs are concerned.

  • The third scenario, less likely than the previous one but perhaps more likely than the first, is that the S&P 500 will collapse by 30% to 4,300 points. In this scenario, the trade war must escalate, and the European Union must enact a particularly disadvantageous tariff plan against the United States. At that point, there must be an even more aggressive reaction from Trump, both against the European Union and against China. The whole thing will last several months.
  • The fourth scenario, the least likely, is the same as the third, except that it will last longer. The tariffs will be in place for several quarters without any agreement being reached between the United States and the targeted countries, and this will significantly weaken the U.S. economy. In this scenario, the risk of stagflation is high, as the tariffs create inflationary pressure. The Fed won't even be able to reduce rates and the S&P 500 will lose 40%.

I don't think a scenario worse than the fourth is feasible, but anything can happen. The only certainty is that, even if this were to be a bear market, sooner or later, there will be a recovery. Investing gradually during a bear market guarantees a faster recovery, and before the S&P 500 reaches new highs, you'll already be in profit. Usually, two years of patience is more than enough to not even realize that there has been a recession along the way.

data.title: Nike: Reciprocal Tariffs More Likely To Change 'Win Now' Strategy To 'Win Never'
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about NIKE, Inc. (NYSE:NKE), back in July 2024, I analysed the company’s Q4 earnings report and argued how the company’s innovation strategy had nothing to show for, and why the company was risking falling further behind the competition. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, the stock has tumbled nearly 24%, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which has declined 7% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the extent of the impact that the recent announcement of the reciprocal tariffs would have on the company’s long-term growth, and whether its turnaround strategy can shield it from some tariff-related headwinds.

Nike’s Turnaround Attempt Just Got A Whole Lot Harder

If one carefully listened to NKE’s Q3 earnings call, then it becomes very clear that the management really tried their best to demonstrate a lot of optimism about the company’s future. New CEO, Elliott Hill, about 6 months into the job, talked about how, after studying the current state of the company, he and his team have outlined a new turnaround strategy dubbed “Win Now.” The strategy would prioritize five core areas (running, basketball, football, training, and sportswear), and across three countries (U.S., China, and the United Kingdom) in general, and five cities (New York, Los Angeles, London, Beijing, and Shanghai) in particular. The ‘Win Now’ strategy will also focus on a complete product refresh by focusing on new product launches, in order to offset the continuous declines seen in the company’s flagship products such as Air Force 1 and Jordan 1. Finally, the strategy also focuses on a clear goal of rebuilding relationships with the company’s wholesale partners, and refining its marketplace presence, especially in China, in order to improve customer engagement.

The most recent quarter of NKE came in better-than-expected, with a beat on both the top and bottom lines, but all key metrics showed significant declines. I am particularly concerned about gross margins, which were down 330 bps (3.3%) and diluted EPS, which were down by a whopping 30% y/y. Moreover, while inventories were down 2% y/y, they still came in at $7.5 billion, which suggests that NKE has a lot of work ahead to clear out their old merchandise before they can even consider scaling up their new products. There was some indication that this might be the case during the Q3 earnings call, when management announced that the production of Pegasus Premium is only expected to scale up by the fall of 2025, and new additions to the Vomero franchise are only expected to arrive at some time during next fiscal year. Finally, the pain from China continued, as revenues declined 15% y/y on a constant currency basis, and EBIT in the region declined by 42% y/y.

Despite these dismal figures, and despite issuing softer-than-expected guidance, the management, during the earnings call, was buzzing with optimism and reiterated their confidence in the success of the Win Now strategy. This is not the first time that NKE management has demonstrated irrational optimism, and it is unfortunate that despite being in a harsher macro environment today, the optimism doesn’t seem to subside.

Investors, in my opinion, shouldn’t fall for this optimism, especially now that the reciprocal tariffs have been announced by the Trump administration. These tariffs have, according to me, made NKE’s turnaround strategy likely to be dead on arrival. There are multiple reasons behind my thesis. Firstly, the company’s guidance for Q4 did not take into account the reciprocal tariffs, which came in much harsher than expected. Q4 gross margins, for instance, are expected to be down 400 to 500 bps (4% to 5%) y/y, as a result of the transition to the Win Now strategy. The gross margins guidance only factored in the initial tariffs that were imposed on China and Mexico. Since then, the tariffs on China have been bumped up from 20% to 54%. To make matters worse, approximately 80% of Nike’s imports to the United States come from Vietnam, which just got slapped with a 46% tariff. Another 6% of Nike’s imports to the United States come from Indonesia, which got slapped with 32% reciprocal tariffs. While only about 7% of Nike's imports to the U.S. come from China, the new 54% tariffs make it a costly affair. It is, therefore, not surprising to me that the company tanked 14.5% post the reciprocal tariffs announcement.

I am not saying that NKE's management should have accurately predicted the extent of the Trump Administration’s reciprocal tariffs. Nobody anticipated that they would come in harsher-than-expected, especially based on the signals that White House officials were sending in the days prior to the announcement. But to proclaim that the 'Win Now' strategy’s success is a foregone conclusion when there was also the possibility that the macroenvironment would be getting worse, should not offer any reassurance to investors. Moreover, the demand creation expense for the latest quarter was up 8% y/y, which makes the level of confidence that the management has placed on this strategy, even more shocking. NKE wants to win now. However, the extent of reciprocal tariffs on the company’s major import locations should make investors wonder investors whether NKE would ever win.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Target Price

$43

Forward P/E

17.2x

Projected FY25 Diluted EPS

$2.34

Forward PEG Ratio

3.36

Projected FY26 EPS Growth

5.12%

Projected FY26 EPS

$2.46

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Seeking Alpha, Author’s Calculations & NKE Q3FY25 Earnings Call.

The company now expects Q4 revenues to be down mid-teens range, “albeit at the low-end.” With the macroeconomic environment at its worst in a while for consumer discretionary names, I have assumed Q4 revenues to decline 16% y/y, higher than management’s own guidance. This would result in projected Q4 revenues to be $10.59 billion.

The company now expects gross margins in Q4 to decline to 400 to 500 bps. The guidance doesn’t take into account the impact of reciprocal tariffs, and as such, I have assumed FY25 gross margins to decline by 500 bps y/y, which would result in projected gross margins of 39.7% for Q4, subsequently resulting in Q4 gross profits of $4.2 billion.

The company expects Q4 SG&A expenses to be up low to mid-single digits. I have assumed a 2% increase, resulting in Q4 SG&A expenses of $3.78 billion. Other income and expenses are expected to be $45 million to $55 million. I have assumed Q4's other income and expenses, including net interest income to be $55 million. I have assumed the effective tax rate to be 15.7% for Q4. Combining all these figures would result in Q4 of net income after taxes of $0.475 billion. At 1.481 billion diluted shares outstanding, Q4 diluted EPS is projected to come in at $0.32. This results in a total projected FY25 Diluted EPS of $2.34.

According to LSEG Data, the company currently trades at a forward P/E of 26.9x, making it the most expensive among its peers. NKE is a market leader but faces significantly more challenges in the current climate compared to some of its other peers such as LULU. For instance, LULU’s China business is doing remarkably well compared to NKE. And while both companies have exposure to countries like Vietnam, the fact that LULU’s turnaround story is at a more advanced level compared to NKE, which has barely got started suggests that it doesn’t deserve to trade at current multiples. In my most recent article on LULU, I put a forward P/E of 22.8x for the company. NKE’s trailing P/E stands at 17.2x, according to LSEG Data. I find this to be a more reasonable assumption under the current climate. Therefore, I have assumed this multiple for my calculations.

NKE currently trades at a forward PEG ratio of 11.23, according to Seeking Alpha, which makes it significantly overvalued relative to the industry median (1.26) and relative to its own 5-year historical average (3.36). At its historical 5-year forward PEG ratio of 3.36, EPS growth would come in at 5.12%, and FY26 EPS is subsequently projected to come in at $2.46.

A forward P/E of 17.2x and a projected EPS of $2.46 would result in a price target of $43, which suggests that the stock is currently overvalued by nearly 23% at current levels. To make matters worse, the company’s momentum is significantly broken, as seen by the D+ grade on Seeking Alpha's Momentum scale. Combining all of this, I am downgrading the stock from a HOLD to a SELL.

Risk Factors

The main risk to my bear case is that the impact of the tariffs would turn out to be relatively lower compared to what is currently being expected. At the time of writing, news has been trickling in, which mentions that President Trump has had positive talks with Vietnam, and this could see tariffs being lowered on the country. This is a positive, and if implemented, could see the company getting a significant boost to its margins.

The other risk factor is that the company can clear its existing inventory at lower markdowns than current expectations. Once again this would boost margins and subsequently the company’s bottom line.

Concluding Thoughts

NKE has undergone quite a transformation since I last wrote about the company. CEO John Donahoe is no longer at the helm, and there is a real sense of emergency from the current management about undertaking a long-overdue product refresh. The Q3 earnings call was one where the new CEO Elliott Hill was expressing a lot of confidence in the company’s latest turnaround strategy, dubbed ‘Win Now.’ While the strategy sounds promising, there is a long way to go before the company can get back on the growth track in my opinion. The existing products continued to be sold at a discount, thereby affecting the company’s margins, and the $7.5 billion in inventory suggests that there is a long way to go before they can be cleared. While the new products have been well-received, they are not likely to change the company’s trajectory in the near term.

And then there’s the reciprocal tariffs, the impact of which, the management simply hasn’t considered in the guidance. These tariffs have come in far worse than expected, as a result of which, the company’s future growth is likely to be adversely affected. From a valuation standpoint, the stock continues to be overvalued even at current levels. What is so surprising to see is the hyper-optimism that the management continues to exude, despite all these headwinds. The last time such levels of misplaced optimism were projected, the CEO ended up losing his job. Existing investors must have a sense of déjà vu, especially after the Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Sangamo Therapeutics: Scope Goes Beyond That Of Recent Eli Lilly Licensing Deal
data.description:
Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMO) just the other day announced that it had been able to establish a deal with Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) in order to advance up to 5 neurological target indications. This big pharma specifically wants to develop genomic medicines to be delivered to the central nervous system [CNS] efficiently using Sangamo's proprietary STAC-BBB intravenously delivered capsid. This deal is another good one for this small-cap biotech because it brings about an upfront payment of about $18 million, and it is then eligible to earn up to $1.4 billion in licensed target fees and milestone payments across 5 target indications. The stock gapped higher the other day in after-hours trading on the back of the release of such news, but today it is trading flat with the broader market.

However, I believe that there is another even more important reason to go over this company now. Not just because it established a third deal with a big pharmaceutical company to develop the STAC-BBB AAV capsid to treat a wide variety of disorders. I believe that while this capsid is primed to deliver strong growth in the future if it can be delivered into the CNS of humans effectively, there is a better near-term opportunity that investors should keep an eye on. This would be in terms of the development of its Fabry Disease program, which uses gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec to treat these patients. The reason why investors should care about this program now is because there is a major catalyst event on the horizon which is going to have a huge impact on changing the scope of the company's value.

This would be in terms of the fact that it expects to release data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study, using isaralgagene civaparvovec to treat these Fabry Disease patients, in the 1st half of 2025. The significance of this is that the FDA already agreed that 52-week estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] data of this caliber could be used as an interim endpoint so that it could file for Accelerated Approval of this gene therapy. If the data pans out, then the company notes that it will be able to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] to the FDA in the 2nd half of 2025. Another catalyst is that the company continues to engage in business development discussions so that it could possibly establish a partnership to help commercialize it just in case it is ultimately approved for U.S. marketing.

Fabry Disease Program Remains On Track With 1st Half 2025 Data Expected

As I stated above, while Sangamo likely has a good pathway forward with several of the big pharma collaboration agreements it had established for the advancement of its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV Capsid, it has a near-term catalyst that is likely to drive shareholder value. Of course, that's if the program ends up with a positive clinical outcome. This would be in terms of the development of gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease, which is being explored in the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. Before going over recent data from this mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to be released for this program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Fabry Disease [FD] is characterized as a rare genetic disorder by which these patients are unable to produce a specific enzyme that is important for breaking down a fat-like substance in the patient's body known as sphingolipids. This is classified as a lysosomal storage disorder in which patients lack the important enzyme of alpha-galactosidase A [alpha-GAL]. Why is the lack of this enzyme a very bad thing? It is because without this enzyme in place to break down such fats, then they are deposited on the tissues and blood vessels in many parts of the body. Such parts of the body that could be affected by Fabry Disease are as follows:

  • Kidneys.
  • Brain.
  • Central Nervous System.
  • Skin.
  • Heart.

The global Fabry Disease market is expected to grow to $5.17 billion by 2034. This is indeed a very large market opportunity and as I will explain below, the company might actually have an Accelerated Approval pathway, depending upon what happens next with the ongoing trial.

In order to see whether or not Sangamo Therapeutics will be able to effectively use isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of these patients with Fabry Disease, it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. This specific trial is set to recruit up to a total of 34 patients who are to receive a single dose of this gene therapy to treat their disease. The way this study was set up was to look at safety primarily at first, over a 12-month period. However, there is an efficacy endpoint that is being evaluated for this particular trial, which is the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. Before going over initial data from this study, I believe it is important to note two things here, which are why this endpoint is being done and the reason why it could be used to move isaralgagene civaparvovec towards the possibility of filing a BLA of it in the 2nd half of 2025.

A problem with Fabry Disease is that the fatty deposits on tissues and blood vessels of organs and other parts of the body cause damage. One hallmark problem that occurs in these patients is that without proper treatment, these patients can have end-stage renal disease [ESRD]. In order to measure the stabilization of kidney disease for these patients, the eGFR slope is utilized. A therapy is considered effective when it can slow the decline to a certain point and then ineffective when the slope dramatically falls off. That is, there is the occurrence of eGFR loss that remains ≥3 ml/min/1.73 m2/year (≥2.5 ml/min/1.73 m2/year in females).

It was revealed in a follow-up of 23 patients who completed 12 months of treatment with isaralgagene civaparvovec that they had seen a positive mean estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) slope of 3.061 mL/min/1.73m2/year. In essence, it indicated that these patients experienced notable improvements in renal function. What remains to be seen now is that longer follow-up, plus the addition of other patients, establishes a stabilization of less of a decline in eGFR. Only if this company gets positive data can it hope to be able to file a BLA of this gene therapy for the treatment of these Fabry Disease patients.

The thing is that the endpoint to be looked at under the Accelerated Approval pathway will be the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. The company had to work with the FDA to get to this point, of allowing this to be an intermediate clinical endpoint that would allow for it to file for Accelerated Approval of this therapy for the treatment of these patients. That is, it met with the FDA in a Type B interaction, whereby the agency stated that such 52-week data from this phase 1/2 STAAR study would be enough to serve as the ability for it to file its BLA. Other positives established from this particular study is that 18 out of 18 patients remain off enzyme replacement therapy [ERT]. Plus, sustained increase of alpha-GAL for up to 47 months in the longest patient treated.

This is where the major catalyst comes into play, which, I believe, will be a huge inflection point for this company.
