That is, it is expected to release eGFR slope 52-week data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study in these Fabry Disease patients, in the 1st half of 2025. The hope is that isaralgagene civaparvovec will be able to stabilize or improve patients in terms of this eGFR slope efficacy measure. If the slope has a massive decline and such patients aren't stabilized, then that could spell an end to this clinical program.

Should the data turn out to be positive, then the company expects to file its BLA for this gene therapy in the 2nd half of 2025. Another potential catalyst, which may or may not happen, would be that it is looking to establish a collaboration agreement with a big pharmaceutical partner to help commercialize this drug. If this happens, then this is another key event that might cause the stock price to trade higher. Plus, it would take care of Sangamo's cash concerns as well.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Sangamo Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $41.9 million in cash of $41.9 million as of December 31st, 2024. The thing is that despite this cash on hand noted, plus additional cash of $10.1 million from an ATM in 2025 and $5 million end of March from Pfizer Inc. (PFE) for $5 million, it doesn't have an extensive cash runway. With this, it guided that it would only have enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least until the middle of Q2 of 2025.

Truth be told, the deal the other day with Eli Lilly only brought in an upfront payment of $18 million. Barring another unforeseen deal regarding the Fabry Disease program or other program in its pipeline, it may need to raise cash. It could probably even do so after the release of data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study targeting patients with Fabry Disease.

The company burns roughly $33.5 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $23.6 million in R&D expenses and then $9.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sangamo Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the collaboration agreement it had made with several big pharma companies to develop its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV capsid. The belief is that this intravenously delivered capsid can cross the blood-brain barrier and achieve transduction in the brain in a more efficient manner in the CNS. The risk here is that there is no assurance that these findings will be replicated in human clinical trials, nor such capsids will be able to achieve success in such ongoing studies. With this capsid failing to achieve proof-of-concept, this means that Sangamo would lose out on being able to obtain milestone payments and royalties on future net sales of products marketed. Not only that, but such trial failures would cause the stock price to trade lower.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the Accelerated Approval pathway of the Fabry Disease program, which I went over above. The hope is that isaralgagene civaparvovec as gene therapy will be effective in being able to help these patients in the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. In particular, the goal is to achieve the intermediate endpoint of the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. The risk here is that upon the release of data, there is no guarantee that it will end up being positive. Even if a BLA filing does happen for isaralgagene civaparvovec for these patients, there is no assurance that the FDA will grant marketing approval for it upon review.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the advancement of giroctocogene fitelparvovec for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe Hemophilia A. Pfizer chose to terminate its collaboration agreement with Sangamo for this program. The hope is that this small-cap biotech can find another partner that is willing to fund it going forward. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will end up finding a partner who is willing to continue to advance it. Another risk to consider is that upon no suitors being found to fund it, it could just choose to end the program entirely.

Conclusion

Sangamo Therapeutics has done well to develop the use of its gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. It is nearing the finish line for this program and the upcoming eGFR slope 52-week data in the 1st half of 2025 will be a huge inflection point. In order for the other two catalysts to happen, they have to be positive. With positive data, that will first lead to the ability to file a BLA of this gene therapy to treat these patients. Secondly, the company would then still be inclined to establish a partnership to help commercialize this drug. Beyond the scope of the Fabry Disease, Hemophilia A treatment programs, and the collaborations with big pharma for the STAC-BBB capsid, it has an extensive pipeline with another candidate.

One such other candidate would be ST-503 which is being developed to treat pain due to idiopathic small fiber neuropathy [iSFN]. This gene therapy specifically targets the SCN9A gene and the purpose of this is to reduce the expression of NAV1.7 sodium channels in sensory neurons. In essence, with this being fully established, it is supposed to have a huge impact on reducing pain hypersensitivity after patients only receive one intrathecal administration of ST-503. With the potential for Accelerated Approval of isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease based on the intermediate clinical endpoint, plus several big pharma deals signed to develop its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV capsid, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cincinnati Financial: Being A Dividend King Not Enough To Offset Cyclical Headwinds
data.description:
Cincinnati Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:CINF) is an insurance company with a great dividend history, but this doesn't seem to be a big support for its share price ahead. As this is my first coverage on the company, in this article I review its business profile and most recent earnings, to see if CINF is an attractive play for long-term investors or not in the insurance sector.

Business Overview

CINF operates as a property and casualty (P&C) insurance company, being among the 25 largest P&C carriers in the country based on annual net written premiums. The company is based in Ohio and was founded in 1950, having therefore a long history in the insurance industry. Its current market value is about $22 billion, being a mid-sized insurance company by this measure.

While its core business is providing P&C insurance, it also offers life insurance, leasing and financing services to its customers. As shown in the next graph, the company's revenues are well diversified by insurance lines, with commercial lines being the largest business segment.

Regarding its business model, CINF operates mainly through independent insurance agents, which distribute its products and services throughout 46 states. In the life insurance segment, it also offers leasing and financing services, which is not usual among P&C companies. The company believes that by operating in this way, it can be more profitable and grow sustainably over the long term, leaving significant decision-making to local subsidiaries that can better adapt product offering and underwriting to specific market needs.

Regarding its growth strategy, CINF aims to offer a good service to agents, expecting to build long-term relationships and gain more market share within its selected agents. On top of business growth, the company also seeks to operate profitably in the insurance segment, by having strong underwriting expertise and knowledge which should lead to adequate pricing throughout market cycles.

Historically, this strategy has been sound given that CINF's growth history is positive, reporting a compounded annual growth rate (CGAR) of 8% on P&C premiums over the past 15 years, beating the industry's growth during this period. On the other hand, its combined ratio was 96% on average over the past 15 years, which is not an impressive outcome compared to some of its peers.

Indeed, as I've covered in previous articles, there are P&C companies that consistently report combined ratios near or below 90% over the long term, such as Chubb (CB) or Progressive (PGR), showing that CINF does not stand out in the P&C industry by its underwriting capabilities. Nevertheless, CINF has been able to report underwriting profits over the past few years that were better than the industry average, but due to a relatively low underwriting profitability, most of its value creation in the past has been generated by financial investments rather than its core insurance business.

As shown in the previous graph, CINF's historical value creation has been driven mainly by equity portfolio gains and investment income, while P&C underwriting has relatively a small contribution to value creation. This happens because CINF has a different asset allocation than most insurance companies, investing a large part of its investment portfolio in equities. Indeed, about 40% of its allocation is to equities, while usually insurance companies are much more conservative in their investment allocation, preferring to invest most of their investments in low-risk assets classes, such as sovereign, municipal, or credit bonds. Usually, fixed-income securities represent between 80-90% of insurance companies' investment allocation, while riskier asset classes have much lower weights.

This is clearly a distinctive factor for CINF compared to other insurance companies, making it to some extent comparable to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), that also invests considerable own funds in equities. Moreover, CINF's investment style also seems to be biased to value investing, even though it also has sizable holdings in companies like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) or Broadcom (AVGO) in the technology sector. Nevertheless, its large exposure to equities makes its investment case more geared to the capital markets than most of its peers, which can be a double-edge sword, depending on the stock market being on a bull or bear market trend.

In recent years, this profile has not been particularly positive, as CINF's share price returns have been lower than compared to the P&C industry in the U.S. and the overall stock market, as shown in the next graph. This should raise some questions about its business model and investment allocation, as the company has been a laggard compared to peers.

However, the company is not expected to change much its business strategy going forward given that its main targets are to invest in its insurance business, invest more in equity securities, deliver a growing dividend, repurchase shares if capitalization remains sound, and eventually pursue M&A transactions if attractive opportunities arise.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, CINF has reported a positive operating momentum in the recent past as the P&C market has been on a "hard" cycle, which means pricing has improved and claims have remained relatively low, leading to improved profitability for most players in the industry.

CINF was no exception and reported higher premiums, investment income, and earnings last year, while its book value reached a new record high. This positive performance was supported both by its insurance business and positive investment returns, showing that is operating backdrop was quite positive during 2024.

Indeed, its total revenues amounted to nearly $11.4 billion last year, up by 13% YoY, driven both by higher net written premiums (+12% YoY to $8.9 billion) and investment income (+15% YoY to more than $1 billion). Regarding investment income, the company reached a significant milestone given that it surpassed more than $1 billion for the first time, as CINF investment portfolio has increased both due to higher earnings and positive returns on the capital markets over the past couple of years, to more than $28 billion at the end of 2024.

Regarding its insurance operations, profitability improved due to higher premiums growth than loss and underwriting expenses growth during the past year, resulting in an underwriting profit of $580 million in 2024 (+45% YoY). Its combined ratio was 93.4% last year (vs. 94.9% in 2023), a positive trend that accelerated in the last two quarters of the year as premium growth was clearly higher than loss expenses. In Q4 2024, its combined ratio improved to 84.7%, showing that its underwriting profitability improved markedly toward the year-end. Its net income increased by 24% YoY to nearly $2.3 billion last year, of which close to $1.1 billion were capital gains related to its investment portfolio.

While this was a positive performance and the operating landscape remains favorable for insurance companies, the company was exposed to the LA wildfires back in January, which will impact negatively its underwriting result in Q1 2025. The company's guidance is to report a pre-tax catastrophe loss between $450-525 million net of recoveries, being a significant hit for the company's loss ratio in 2025. Indeed, if this event happened last year, the company would still make an underwriting profit, but it would be quite low. This shows that LA wildfires were a significant event and the company's underwriting profitability will be much lower in 2025 compared to its recent history.

Indeed, according to analysts' estimates, CINF's combined ratio is expected to be around 99.2% in 2025. Thus, the company is expected to report only a small underwriting profit this year, being a significant headwind for earnings growth in the short term. Moreover, the company's relatively high exposure to equities is also concerning given recent market volatility and declining prices in recent weeks, boding badly for investment gains in the first half of 2025. While current consensus seems to reflect higher catastrophe losses, considering that CINF is expected to report a net loss of $40 million in Q1 2025, lower equity prices doesn't seem to be reflected year as book value is expected to grow in Q2, something that may be difficult to achieve due to recent weakness in the stock market that will impact the company's comprehensive income.

Despite these cyclical headwinds and prospects of lower earnings in 2025, the company has recently raised its dividend. Investors can see this as a sign that management is confident about the business outlook, but this seems to be more a reflection of its past performance rather than being particularly bullish about its financial figures ahead. Moreover, investors should consider that CINF is a dividend king, which means it has raised its dividend for more than 50 years consecutively, a trend that its management is clearly committed to maintain regardless of its earnings path.

As shown in the next graph, its dividend history seems to be more a "political" commitment rather than a reflection of its earnings, as the company raised its dividend even during years when it reported negative earnings, showing that the relationship between earnings and dividends can be questionable in the short term.

Having said that, its dividend has been covered by earnings over the long term, given that its dividend payout ratio was only 29% on average over the last decade. Thus, its dividend seems to be sustainable, and I'm not expecting the company to give up on its dividend growth policy ahead, but investors should be aware that its fantastic dividend history is not much likely to support its share price in the short term. Furthermore, its dividend yield is only about 2.5%, which is not particularly impressive, and therefore I don't expect its terrific dividend history to be enough to offset cyclical headwinds in the coming quarters.

Regarding its valuation, CINF is currently trading at around 1.5x book value, which is in-line with its historical valuation over the past five years. This means its shares appear to be fairly valued right now, following a 10% correction over the past few days due to the trade tariffs drama that is clearly not specific to CINF.

Conclusion

Cincinnati Financial Corporation has reported a positive operating performance over the past couple of years, but this trend is expected to change in 2025 due to higher catastrophe losses and recent market turmoil is also negative for its investment portfolio. While some investors may see recent market weakness as a buying opportunity, I don't see CINF as being undervalued and don't think it offers much value for long-term investors right now.
